result:
I keep thinking about the way light looks when it’s almost gone—how it clings to the edges of things like it’s afraid of falling off. Not sunset, not quite; more like the moment after, when the sky has already swallowed the sun and the world is holding its breath. Colors don’t fade so much as surrender, quietly, without argument. I used to chase that light with a camera, back when I believed you could trap something authentic inside four edges of glass and silver. Now I know the best you can do is borrow it for a heartbeat, and even then it leaks out the sides.

There’s a crack in my bedroom wall that looks like the Mississippi if you squint. I found it the first night I moved into this apartment, while the movers were thumping boxes down like they were punishing them for the weight. I stood there barefoot, imagining water flowing through the plaster, paddle steamers and muddy banks, Huck Finn waving from a crooked nail. I told myself I’d spackle it, paint over it, make the wall whole again. Three years later it’s still there, wider by a hair’s width every winter. I’ve grown protective of it, the way you grow protective of anything that stays when everything else leaves.

Memory works like that crack: starts small, expands invisibly, until one day you realize the blueprint inside your head doesn’t match the outside world anymore. My grandmother’s kitchen used to be enormous—high sunlit windows, a wooden table long enough for a holiday cavalry charge. When I drove past the house last spring I found it had shrunk, coughed up into condos, the lot subdivided and neutered. I parked across the street and tried to reconcile the two versions, like attempting to lay a transparency over reality and discovering nothing lined up. A kid zipped by on a scooter, scattering dandelion ghosts. He didn’t know he was trespassing across a kingdom that once had room for twenty cousins and a Labrador mix named Senator.

Sometimes I think we’re all just walking backward into the future, eyes fixed on some vanished skyline. We collect artifacts—ticket stubs, coffee mugs, the perfume of people we loved once that lingers in a sweater cuff—because we’re convinced the right arrangement will spell a spell, open a door, bring the dead hours back to life. My phone stores 42,317 photos. Swipe far enough and I’m 19 again, cheeks sunburned on a ferry deck, believing immortality was a matter of refusal: if I simply declined to blink, the moment could not expire. The ferry dissolved into scrap metal years ago. The pixels endure, bright little liars insisting nothing changes.

Yet everything does. Even the words I’m typing right now are drifting away from me, electrons skating across servers I’ll never visit, translated into voltages and back again, altered by your screen, your room light, the fingerprint smudge on your lens. When you read “sunburn” you won’t picture the precise coral-red I wore for three days, won’t feel the sting of wind off Puget Sound. Language is a suitcase that always arrives with its contents rumpled, smelling of someone else’s cologne. Still we keep packing, zipping, checking the bag onto the next carousel. The alternative is too lonely to consider.

I read once that the universe is expanding at 74 kilometers per second per megaparsec. I have no idea what a megaparsec looks like, but I picture it like those expandable garden hoses: one moment tidy, the next moment writhing across the lawn, knocking over the sprinkler and the neighbor’s patience. Space itself pulling away from itself, every point the center of a private exodus. I feel that in my chest some nights, a gentle spaghettification, like I’m being thinned into astronomical noodles. I lie very still and listen to the walls, the way they creak in syllables that almost form my name. The crack in the plaster widens another micron; somewhere a star runs out of excuses and collapses. Same process, different scale.

Yesterday I found a cicada shell on the sidewalk, paper-thin, clinging to the concrete by microscopic claws. I crouched, watched it tremble in the breeze, a ghost of itself. Seventeen years underground, a single month overhead, then gone—yet the shell stays, a borrowed silhouette. I thought of carrying it home, placing it beside the Mississippi crack, two maps of departure in dialogue. But I left it there. Someone else will pass, maybe a kid who’ll pocket it and turn it into an astronaut for a Lego launchpad, maybe a dog that’ll sniff once and forget. Either way, the shell will crumble, the concrete will buckle, and the continent will drift two centimeters farther from whatever it’s drifting from. Nothing lost, nothing gained, only relocated.

We’re told to live in the present, as if the present were a country we could obtain a visa to. I’ve never managed the paperwork. My present keeps slipping into anecdote, the way a candle flame keeps slipping up the wick. I smell cedar and I’m twelve at summer camp, learning to whittle while secretly hoping the cute counselor notices the careful curl of wood. I taste cinnamon and I’m twenty-eight in a hospital corridor, clutching bitter vending-machine coffee while my father forgets my name one room away. Sensory smugglers, hijacking the now into someplace we’ve already been.

Yet every so often the curtain tears. Last week the elevator in my building jammed between floors for seven minutes. No cell signal, the emergency bulb flickering like a nervous interrogator. My fellow passenger was a stranger, mid-thirties, wearing a lanyard from the courthouse across the street. We stood in enforced silence, eyes politely avoiding the shared intimacy of panic. Then the lights steadied, and without thinking I said, “Tell me something you’ve never told anyone.” She looked at me, earrings swaying as the car hiccuped. “I used to eat toothpaste,” she said. “Mint only. Like it was frosting.” I laughed, surprised by the honesty steamrolling convention. She asked for mine in return. “I still don’t know how to be loved,” I answered, the sentence arriving fully formed, like it had been waiting on the threshold. The bell dinged, doors pried open, and we stepped into the hallway as if nothing had happened. I don’t know her name, never will. But in that stalled steel box we held the genuine article—no cameras, no future, just the raw splice of two humans briefly occupying the same oxygen. The moment didn’t need to be captured. It completed itself, like a wave that crests, breaks, and is absorbed back into the larger body of water, unchanged except for the salt it leaves on your lips.

So I guess that’s what I’m chasing now: not the light, not the memory, but the narrow seam where two strangers admit they are afraid of vanishing. A crack wide enough for honesty, small enough that the universe doesn’t notice. Somewhere in the dark the Mississippi keeps flowing inside my wall, carrying ghosts of paddle steamers and childhood summers. The cicada shell turns to dust. The expansion continues, indifferent and magnificent. And I keep typing, borrowing electricity, turning oxygen into vowels, trusting you to finish the sentence inside your chest.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
